era_context: 888 CE marks a critical juncture in European political history: the effective end of the unified Carolingian Empire. Charles III (the Fat), the last emperor to rule a substantial portion of the Frankish realm, died in January 888, triggering the final fragmentation of Charlemagne's legacy. The empire split into competing kingdoms: West Francia, East Francia, and Italy, each with its own claimants and dynasties. Simultaneously, the Viking Age continued to reshape coastal and riverine Europe, while the Islamic world experienced further decentralization under the Abbasid Caliphate.

events.title: Death of Charles III (the Fat); final collapse of unified Carolingian Empire
events.description: Charles III, Holy Roman Emperor and king of the Franks, died on January 13, 888, at Neidingen (modern Donaueschingen, Germany). His death marked the definitive end of Carolingian imperial unity. Charles had inherited the West Frankish kingdom in 884 and briefly reunited much of the Frankish realm, but his authority was weak and contested. His death triggered immediate succession disputes: West Francia, East Francia, and Italy each sought independent rulers, and the Carolingian line fragmented into competing branches. This event is often marked as the symbolic end of the Carolingian Empire and the beginning of the medieval feudal order.
key_figures: Arnulf of Carinthia (nephew, claimed East Francia)
events.certainty_note: The death of Charles III on January 13, 888, is well-attested in multiple near-contemporary chronicles. The date is secure.

events.title: Berengar II crowned King of Italy
events.description: Following Charles III's death, Berengar II, Marquess of Friuli, was crowned King of Italy. Berengar's claim was contested by other Italian magnates, particularly Guy of Spoleto, leading to a succession dispute that would dominate Italian politics for the next decade. Berengar's reign was marked by conflict with the papacy and with rival claimants, and he would eventually be deposed by Otto I in 961.
events.certainty_note: Berengar's coronation is attested in chronicles, but the exact date (888 vs. 889) is debated by modern scholars. The event is secure; the precise year is approximate.

events.title: Guy of Spoleto crowned King of Italy (rival claim)
events.description: Guy of Spoleto, a powerful Italian magnate, also claimed the Italian throne following Charles III's death. Guy's coronation created a direct conflict with Berengar II's claim, and both men sought papal recognition and military support. This dual coronation reflects the fragmentation of Italian authority and the power of regional magnates. Guy would eventually gain the upper hand and be crowned Holy Roman Emperor in 891.
key_figures: Guy of Spoleto (crowned king of Italy)
events.certainty_note: Guy's coronation is attested, but the exact date (888 vs. 889) is uncertain. Modern scholarship places it in 888 or early 889.

disconfirming_evidence: Modern scholarship has questioned the traditional narrative of 888 as a sudden 'collapse' of the Carolingian Empire. Some historians argue that the empire had already fragmented de facto in the 840s–870s, and 888 merely formalized existing political realities. The 'election' of new kings in 888–889 was not a democratic process but a negotiation among aristocratic factions. Additionally, the significance of Charles III's death has been somewhat overstated in older historiography; his reign was already weak and contested, and his death did not immediately resolve succession disputes—conflicts continued for years. No disconfirming evidence identified for other events listed.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 888 is moderate to rich, but heavily weighted toward West and East Francia and Italy. Sources are primarily near-contemporary chronicles (Regino of Prüm, Annals of Fulda, Liutprand of Cremona) compiled within a generation or two of events. These sources reflect the perspective of the Frankish and Italian ecclesiastical establishment and may overemphasize the role of the papacy and underrepresent the agency of lay nobility and regional powers. The record for the Islamic world, Byzantium, and Asia is thinner in Western sources. Succession disputes are often vaguely dated, and modern scholars continue to debate the precise chronology of coronations in 888–889.

graph_edges.note: Charles III's death triggered Berengar II's claim to the Italian throne.
Charles III's death triggered Guy of Spoleto's claim to the Italian throne.
Berengar II and Guy of Spoleto made competing claims to the same throne, creating a succession dispute.
Guy of Spoleto's successful claim to Italy enabled his later imperial coronation.
